title: Thinking Biblically About Life: Trauma and Suffering
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-10-24 - Thinking Biblically About Life 2 - Trauma and Suffering - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Two Foundational Presuppositions
subpoints: Scripture is sufficient for all issues concerning faith and practice (2 Peter 1)
What the Bible calls sin is always sin, even when the body makes it harder to obey
The body can make obedience more difficult but cannot cause sin — personal responsibility remains

outline.title: Defining Trauma
subpoints: Trauma is the emotional, spiritual, and physical disruption caused by being overwhelmed by extreme suffering
Can be a single traumatic event or a repeated series of experiences (abuse, war, etc.)
PTSD: a reaction to what was witnessed, not necessarily what was done — not a disorder, a human response
Common signs: flashbacks, insomnia, panic attacks, severe anxiety, depression, guilt

outline.title: Problems with Trauma-Informed Counseling
subpoints: Puts trauma at the center of all explanation — removes personal responsibility and hope for change
Removes the heart from the center (Proverbs 4:23) and installs trauma instead
Available therapies (CBT, EMDR, etc.) may improve functioning but cannot produce Christlikeness
The DNA of secular methodologies carries rotten foundational presuppositions that cannot simply be borrowed

outline.title: What Kind of People Should We Be to Help Sufferers?
subpoints: Patient, biblically-minded, kind, compassionate, forgiving, service-minded, prayerful, encouraging
Be 'China-kind' people — those others trust with their most breakable things
Focused on others; attentive; gentle; good listeners (Proverbs 18)

outline.title: How Scripture's Sufferers Handled Trauma
subpoints: Cried out to God in prayer (Psalm 62: 'pour out your heart before Him')
Practiced disciplined thinking — mind stayed on God (Isaiah 26:3)
Put hope in the Lord (Psalms 42-43)
Sang songs of lament (Acts 16: Paul and Silas sang in prison after being beaten)

outline.title: Practical Helps
subpoints: Sing aloud to the Lord — even while suffering; follow Paul and Silas's example
Renew the mind: have Scripture ready to recite; grow in heart awareness
Understand the power of the gospel: Christ was traumatized on the cross and is our sympathetic High Priest (Hebrews 4)
Rest in Christ when sleeping: meditate on hymns and Scripture before sleep
Find peace through right thinking during anxiety attacks (Philippians 4:6-9; Psalm 27:1-3)
Scripture is sufficient: God's word understood and contextually applied is what sufferers need most

summary: In the second installment of the Thinking Biblically About Life adult Sunday school series, Pastor Tito Lyro examines trauma and suffering from a biblical counseling perspective. He reaffirms two foundational presuppositions: the sufficiency of Scripture for all matters of faith and practice, and the unchanging nature of sin even when bodily conditions make obedience more difficult. He then defines trauma carefully — as an emotional, spiritual, and physical disruption caused by extreme suffering — and traces the problems with trauma-informed counseling, which places trauma at the center of all explanation, removes the heart (Proverbs 4:23), eliminates personal responsibility, denies the need for the gospel, and rests on foundational presuppositions that cannot be borrowed piecemeal. Pastor Lyro then describes what kind of people should help suffering believers — patient, compassionate, 'China-kind' people who are trusted with breakable things — and surveys how biblical figures handled traumatic experiences: crying out to God (Psalm 62), disciplining their minds (Isaiah 26:3), putting hope in the Lord (Psalms 42-43), and singing songs of lament (Acts 16). He concludes with concrete, practical helps — singing aloud, mind renewal through Scripture memorization, resting in Christ before sleep, and right thinking during panic attacks — arguing that the church, equipped with God's sufficient Word, has more to offer the traumatized than any secular trauma-informed methodology.
transcript:
## Introduction

We're taking time for a few Sundays to talk about how to think biblically about several different issues in life. The Bible teaches us that we are to be not conformed to this world, but transformed by the renewing of our minds, that we may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God. And part of not being conformed to the world and having our minds renewed is to think biblically about several areas of life, including how we think of suffering.

Last week we talked about how to think biblically about church membership. Next Sunday, Lord willing, we are going to talk about how to think biblically concerning this new idea called gentle parenting. Today I want us to think biblically about suffering and trauma — what the scriptures say concerning those areas.

The mental health world is currently abuzz with the idea of trauma and trauma-informed counseling. It's the current standard of care. But we need to think long and hard before we buy wholesale into this idea.

Does the Bible say anything about trauma? Let me change the question: Does the Bible say anything about suffering? Okay — that's what trauma is. It's suffering. And the Bible talks about suffering quite a bit.

Some say the church should stay out of this area — that biblical counseling is okay for normal discipleship and some bread-and-butter issues, but the serious stuff needs to be sent to a professional. I want to examine that claim.

## Two Foundational Presuppositions

Let's review the two fundamental presuppositions for this series about thinking biblically about issues.

First: **The Scriptures are sufficient for all things concerning faith and practice.** As Peter tells us in 2 Peter 1 — the Scriptures are not only sufficient for how to deal with these issues, but they give us the lens through which we can examine everything else that's out there.

Second: **What the Bible calls sin is always sin, even when the body makes it harder to obey.** For example, anger is sin. But somebody with advancing dementia tends to get angry — are we going to say that anger is no longer sin because of the dementia? That is dangerous territory. The body can make it more difficult to obey God, but it cannot cause us to sin.

## What Is Trauma?

Trauma refers to the emotional, spiritual, and physical disruptions that occur when a person is overwhelmed by extreme suffering. It is the reaction to what happens — not just the event itself. People use the word "traumatized" to describe a person severely impacted by a terrible event such as rape, a natural disaster, or a car accident. An event rises to the level of a traumatic experience when it is sudden and unpredictable, involves a threat to life, or involves a profound violation of trust.

The word also describes a person overwhelmed after a series of experiences — such as childhood abuse, war, or domestic violence.

Regarding PTSD and the military: in World War I it was called "shell shock," in World War II and Vietnam it eventually became "Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder." But many are now dropping the D — it's not a disorder in the sense that these men and women are reacting inappropriately. They are reacting properly to the horrors they saw.

Common signs and experiences of trauma include flashbacks, insomnia, panic attacks and severe anxiety, depression, intrusive thoughts, self-harm, and guilt.

## Problems with Trauma-Informed Counseling

Trauma-informed counseling puts trauma at the center of everything — trauma explains all of a person's problems. What does this approach do? It removes hope, victimizes the person (everything is something that happened to me), releases the person from responsibility, and removes any notion of the gospel, any need for Christ, any hope of change or holiness.

A better way to picture this: instead of trauma at the center, put the heart at the center — because all the issues of life flow from the heart (Proverbs 4:23). The events of life are the "heat" on the outside. The response — whether fruitful or thorny — comes from the heart. The way to change is not primarily changing the heat (which is often impossible) but through the cross, addressing things from a heart ruled by the Lord Jesus Christ.

Available therapies — Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, EMDR, and others — may have some success in helping people feel better and function better. But they do not help anyone become more like Jesus Christ. And they cannot simply be borrowed, because the very DNA of these methodologies carries the foundational presuppositions that they are built upon.

## What Kind of People Should We Be?

What kind of people should we be to help brothers and sisters who are suffering? Patient (1 Thessalonians 5:14), biblically-minded, kind, compassionate, forgiving, service-minded, prayerful, encouraging, willing to provide accountability.

We need to be "China-kind" people — the kind of people others entrust with their most precious, breakable things. We need to be others-focused and tuned in to the needs of others (Philippians 3:2-5). We need to be people who are detailed and focused — good listeners (Proverbs 18).

As the elect of God in Colossians 3:12, we are to put on tender mercies, kindness, humility, meekness, long-suffering — and look where that is applied: in the context of dealing with one another.

## How Did Biblical Figures Handle Trauma?

How do people in Scripture who went through extreme circumstances accomplish what sufferers need?

They **cried out in prayer**. In Psalm 62, in the context of David being betrayed again and again, he says: "My soul waits silently for God alone... Trust in Him at all times, you people. Pour out your heart before Him. God is a refuge for us."

They **practiced a rigorous, disciplined trust** in the Lord. In Isaiah 26, prophesying about the coming Assyrian captivity — a traumatic event by any measure — Isaiah says: "You will keep him in perfect peace, whose mind is stayed on You, because he trusts in You. Trust in the Lord forever."

They **put their hope in the Lord**. That is really the topic of Psalms 42 and 43, where David is staring into the pit of despair. And he says: "Why are you so downcast, O my soul? Hope in God!"

They **sang songs**. A lot of the Psalms are songs of lament. In Acts 16, Paul and Silas had been beaten all day, are in prison, awaiting judgment — and at midnight they were praying and singing hymns to God. That is how they dealt with trauma in their lives.

## Practical Helps

Some practical suggestions for dealing with traumatized responses:

**Sing aloud to the Lord.** It doesn't matter if people think you're crazy. The people in the Bible did this. Paul and Silas sang in the midst of great trauma.

**Renew the spirit of your mind** (Ephesians 4). Mind renewal means coming up with new ways to process life that flow from the Scriptures. In moments of distress, having Scripture ready to recite — which implies memorizing them. The suffering person must also grow in heart awareness: when you feel guilty, ask yourself — why? What is this thought saying about what I believe concerning God's control of all things?

**Understand the power of the gospel.** At the core of the gospel is a traumatizing event — not only the physical suffering of the cross, but the emotionally and spiritually traumatic moment when the Son was separated from the Father for the first time in eternity. It is that Savior who says He is our sympathetic High Priest in Hebrews 4, able to walk with us through trauma.

**Rest in Christ when it is time to sleep.** Singing hymns in your mind and reflecting on the words as you drift into sleep will help your mind stay on Christ. Memorize a hymn like "Be Still, My Soul" and meditate on it at night.

**Find peace through right thinking.** In anxiety attacks, think about what is true about God at that moment. In Philippians 4:8-9, thinking on the right things leads to doing the right things. Psalm 27:1-3 was the passage that helped the pastor through his own panic attack: "The Lord is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?"

## Conclusion

The bottom line, brothers and sisters: the Scriptures are very informed about trauma and they are sufficient to help a brother or sister who has gone through traumatic experiences. Think about Jesus — He is very trauma-informed. He knows what trauma is. He died as a traumatized person (Isaiah 53). Paul in 2 Corinthians 11 catalogues extraordinary suffering and yet remained healthy spiritually and emotionally.

God's Word, understood and contextually applied to sufferers, is what we need to help people overcome their responses to the suffering that happens in their lives. "You shall know the truth and the truth will set you free" (John 8:32). And that is what we are after.

Father, thank you that you are good. We thank you that you speak to us in your Word and that your Word helps us understand everything in life. We pray that you bring us before your presence to worship you this morning. In Jesus' name. Amen.
